items.correct: A group of words from a similar area of meaning — e.g. 'whisper, scream, mumble, shout' all belong to the semantic field of vocalisation.
items.misconception: A semantic field is a list of synonyms for one word.
items.random_wrong: A semantic field is a field of study like physics or biology.
items.why: Synonyms share meaning closely; semantic fields are broader, including related but distinct words. 'Hot' and 'cold' belong to the same semantic field (temperature) but aren't synonyms.

items.q: How does Bruner's view of language acquisition differ from Vygotsky's?
items.correct: Both emphasise social interaction, but Bruner proposes a Language Acquisition Support System (LASS) — adults provide structured, predictable interaction (book-reading, peekaboo) that 'scaffolds' acquisition. Vygotsky's scaffolding is broader (within ZPD across all learning).
items.misconception: Bruner and Vygotsky are saying the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: Bruner was a nativist who opposed all social theories.
items.why: LASS is a specific mechanism (interactive routines) Bruner proposed as the partner to Chomsky's LAD. Students conflate Bruner with Vygotsky, missing the distinctive interactional-routine concept.

items.q: What is a speech act according to Searle/Austin?
items.correct: An utterance that performs an action — promising, ordering, apologising, declaring. Speech acts have locutionary (what's said), illocutionary (intended force) and perlocutionary (effect) dimensions.
items.misconception: A speech act is any time someone speaks.
items.random_wrong: A speech act is a performance or a play.
items.why: Speech act theory analyses how language DOES things, not just describes things. 'I now pronounce you married' is a declaration, not a description. Students miss that utterances can be acts in themselves.

items.q: What does the L in PEEL stand for?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Link. “List” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which reading skill hunts specific facts?
items.why: Correct answer: Scanning. “Skimming” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many letters in PEEL paragraph structure?
items.why: The real figure is 4 letters. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
